coredata.dc:title: Study on formation and photoluminescence of carbon nanowalls grown on silicon substrates by hot filament chemical vapor deposition
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi
Li, Juan
Song, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanowalls (CNWs) were grown on different Si substrates by hot filament chemical vapor deposition, where CH4 was used as the reactive gas. The structure and composition of synthesized CNWs were studied using a field emission scanning electron microscope, a transmission electron microscope, a micro-Raman spectroscope, and an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope. The results indicate that the thick CNWs can be well grown on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots while the thin CNWs are formed on the mirror-polished Si substrate. Beginning with the nucleation of carbon, the formation of CNWs with different structures was studied. The photoluminescence (PL) of CNWs on the two Si substrates was studied using a photoluminescence spectroscope, in which the 325nm line of He–Cd laser was used as the irradiation source. The PL spectra show the two strong blue and green PL peaks centered at about 442 and 549nm and a weak red PL peak centered at about 657nm and the intensity of every PL peak of thin CNWs is stronger than that of thick CNWs. According to the interband transition mechanism of carbon materials, the PL properties of CNWs were analyzed. The outcomes of this study are significant to control the structure of CNWs and contribute to the development of next optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemical vapor deposition
Carbon nanowalls
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and composition of carbon nanowalls

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FESEM image and (b) Raman spectrum of carbon nanodots deposited on the silicon substrate.
FESEM images of CNWs grown on (a) the silicon substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and (b) the mirror-polished silicon substrate.
Raman spectra of CNWs formed on (1) the silicon substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and (2) the mirror-polished silicon substrate.
TEM image of CNWs grown on the silicon substrate deposited with carbon nanodots.
XPS spectra of CNWs formed on (1) the silicon substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and (2) the mirror-polished silicon substrate.

C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of CNWs formed on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate: (a) and (c) the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots; (b) and (d) the mirror-polished Si substrate. (a) C 1s, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s and (d) O 1s.
PL spectra of CNWs formed on (1) the Si substrate deposited with the carbon nanodots and (2) the mirror-polished Si substrate.

head.ce:title: Study on formation and photoluminescence of carbon nanowalls grown on silicon substrates by hot filament chemical vapor deposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical Engineering, Chongqing University of Technology, 69 Hongguang Road, Banan District, Chongqing 400054, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Machine, Chongqing Machinery and Electronic Technician College, Chongqing 400037, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Machinery and Electronic Technician College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanowalls (CNWs) were grown on different Si substrates by hot filament chemical vapor deposition, where CH4 was used as the reactive gas. The structure and composition of synthesized CNWs were studied using a field emission scanning electron microscope, a transmission electron microscope, a micro-Raman spectroscope, and an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope. The results indicate that the thick CNWs can be well grown on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots while the thin CNWs are formed on the mirror-polished Si substrate. Beginning with the nucleation of carbon, the formation of CNWs with different structures was studied. The photoluminescence (PL) of CNWs on the two Si substrates was studied using a photoluminescence spectroscope, in which the 325nm line of He–Cd laser was used as the irradiation source. The PL spectra show the two strong blue and green PL peaks centered at about 442 and 549nm and a weak red PL peak centered at about 657nm and the intensity of every PL peak of thin CNWs is stronger than that of thick CNWs. According to the interband transition mechanism of carbon materials, the PL properties of CNWs were analyzed. The outcomes of this study are significant to control the structure of CNWs and contribute to the development of next optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the formation of Csp1, Csp2 and Csp3 hybridized bonds, carbon can form carbon materials with different structures and properties to have different applications in various areas of mechanics and optoelectronics [1]; thus the carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, carbon nanotips, graphene nanosheets or carbon nanowalls (CNWs) and silicon carbide have been extensively studied [2–5]. In particular, the CNWs have attracted considerable attention in recent years owing to their novel mechanical, optical and electronic properties [5]. To date, the CNWs have mainly been synthesized by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) [5–7] and Ostrikov et al. reviewed the formation of CNWs in a plasma system in detail [8]. The formation model of graphene in plasma suggested by Ostrikov et al. in Ref. [8] provides us a new route of growing CNWs, i.e., the substrate is pre-treated before the CNW growth. In this work, we used plasma-enhanced hot filament CVD (PEHFCVD) to pre-deposit the carbon nanodots on the Si substrate, and then grow the CNWs on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots in the HFCVD system. It is found that the CNWs can better form on the silicon substrate deposited with carbon nanodots than the mirror-polished Si substrate. Beginning with the nucleation of carbon, the formation of CNWs on different substrates was studied. Due to the properties of graphene film related to the layer number of graphene sheets [4], the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CNWs were simultaneously studied using PL spectroscopy. The PL results exhibit that the CNWs generate three PL peaks centered at about 442, 549 and 657nm, which are different with the PL peaks of graphene oxide [9,10]. Depending on the interband transition mechanism of carbon materials, the PL properties of CNWs were analyzed. In this paper, we report the research results on the synthesis and PL properties of CNWs on different Si substrates.

Prior to the growth of carbon nanodots and CNWs, the Si substrates were chemically cleaned with toluene, acetone and ethanol in an ultrasonic bath for 15min to remove the organic residuals, and then they were boiled in a complex solution of NH3, H2O2 and H2O (with a volume ratio of 1:2:5) at 75°C to remove the inorganic residuals. Finally, the Si substrates were repeatedly washed using deionized water for many times.

The carbon nanodots were deposited on the Si substrate in the PEHFCVD system, which is similar to the system described in detail in Ref. [11]. Here the CVD reactor contains the heating and bias systems, in which the heating system constructed by W filaments is used for the decomposition of reactive gases and the heating of substrate and the bias system composed of a DC power supply is used to produce plasma. After the Si substrate was placed in the CVD reactor, the CVD reactor was pumped to a basic pressure of lower than 2Pa, and then a mixture of 30sccm N2 and 70sccm H2 was inlet into the CVD reactor. When the pressure reached about 2000Pa, the AC power supply was turned on to heat the W filaments. Due to the short distance of about 8mm between the filaments and substrate, the substrate was quickly heated to about 850°C. CH4 was inlet into the reactor and the flow rates of CH4, N2 and H2 were adjusted to 20, 30 and 50sccm, respectively. Simultaneously, the DC power supply was turned on and the bias current was set to 160mA to deposit the carbon nanodots for 15min.

The synthesis of CNWs is carried out in the HFCVD system, in which the synthesis procedure is similar to the deposition of carbon nanodots. After the pressure in the CVD reactor was lower than 2Pa, 50sccm H2 was inlet into the CVD reactor and the pressure was adjusted to 2000Pa. When the Si substrate deposited with the carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate were heated in the H2 environment to about 930°C, H2 was replaced by CH4 with the same flow rate as H2 to deposit the CNWs for 7min.

The structure and composition of CNWs were investigated by a Hitachi S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM), a JEOL 2010F transmission electron microscope (TEM), an HR 800 micro-Raman spectroscope and an ESCALab250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS). When the morphologies and structure of CNWs were studied, the FESEM and TEM were operated at 15 and 200kV, respectively. During Raman and XPS measurement, a 532nm line of semiconductor laser and an Al Kα X-ray source were used as the excitation sources for the Raman spectroscope and XPS spectroscope, respectively. The PL of CNWs was measured using an Acton SP2750 photoluminescence spectroscope and the 325nm line of He–Cd laser was used as the excitation source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of carbon nanowalls
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the FESEM image and Raman spectrum of carbon nanodots deposited on the Si substrate. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the carbon nanodots are diversified, and their size is in a range of 14–76nm. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 1(b) shows D and G peaks of carbon materials at about 1341 and 1610cm−1, respectively. From Fig. 1(b), the intensity ratio of G to D peaks is about 1, which indicates that the carbon nanodots have a low degree of graphitization [12].

Fig. 2(a) and (b) shows the FESEM images of CNWs grown on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate. From Fig. 2, one can see that a number of large CNWs can be formed on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots while only some small CNW flakes are formed on the mirror-polished Si substrate. In addition, Fig. 2(a) indicates that the CNWs are formed depending on the carbon nanodots marked by the arrows. According to the typical CNWs, the thickness of CNWs is estimated and it is about 8 and 5nm for the CNWs grown on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate, respectively.

Fig. 3 presents the Raman spectra of CNWs formed on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate. As shown in Fig. 3, every spectrum shows three Raman peaks at about 1349, 1589 and 2704cm−1, which are the D, G and 2D peaks of carbon materials [4]. The strong G and 2D peaks indicate that the synthesized CNWs are graphene flakes [13]. The intensity ratio of 2D to G peaks is 0.74 for spectrum (1) and 1.04 for spectrum (2). These data indicate that the CNWs grown on the mirror-polished Si substrate are thinner than that on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots because the intensity ratio of 2D to G peaks is reduced with increase in the layer number of graphene sheets [14], which is consistent with the SEM results shown in Fig. 2. Comparing spectrum (1) with spectrum (2), the intensity of Raman peaks in spectrum (2) is lower than that in spectrum (1), which further indicates that the thickness of CNWs grown on the mirror-polished Si substrate is smaller than that on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots because the intensity of Raman peak is increased with increase in the scattering volume.

To further confirm the structure of CNWs, the CNWs were studied by TEM after they were scraped from the substrate using a sharp knife. Due to the similarity of Raman spectra of CNWs formed on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate, here the CNWs formed on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots was investigated by TEM. Fig. 4 shows the TEM image of the CNWs grown on the silicon substrate deposited with carbon nanodots, in which the inset shows the high-resolution TEM. The high-resolution TEM indicates that the CNWs are crystalline structure.

Fig. 5 shows the XPS spectra of CNWs formed on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate. As shown in Fig. 5, every spectrum shows the peaks at about 102.6, 151.3, 284.8, and 532.4eV. The two peaks at about 248.8 and 532.4eV are attributed to C 1s and O 1s, respectively. For the two peaks at about 102.6 and 151.3eV, they result from Si 2p and Si 2s, respectively [15].

Fig. 6 shows the C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of CNWs formed on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate, respectively. To study the bond states, the spectra in Fig. 6 are fitted using the standard XPS software after Shirley background subtraction. As shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b), the C 1s XPS spectra are fitted using two peaks, which are at about 284.8 and 285.3eV for spectrum (a) and 284.8 and 285.8eV for spectrum (b). For the three peaks, the peak at about 284.8eV is attributed to the sp2 C–C bond [11] and the two peaks at about 285.3 and 285.8eV are related to sp3 C–C bond [16]. For the O 1s XPS spectra, they are decomposed into two peaks at about 532.3 and 533.1eV, which are associated with C–O–C/C–OH and C–O groups, respectively [17,18].

The formation of differently structured CNWs on different substrates is related to the surfaces of Si substrates. For the Si substrate deposited with the carbon nanodots, the joint of a carbon nanodot with Si surface forms a pit-like defect where a low energy is required for the nucleation of carbon [19]. After methane is decomposed into the hydrocarbon chemical radicals by the hot filaments, carbon easily nucleates in the pit-like defect shown in Fig. 7(a). Due to the formation of carbon nuclei, the edges of carbon nuclei have dangling bonds. As a result, the hydrocarbon chemical radicals join up the edges of carbon nuclei to form a graphene sheet after they diffuse on the Si surface, which is shown in Fig. 7(b). Simultaneously, the hydrocarbon chemical radicals deposited on the carbon dot and nucleus surfaces diffuse toward the graphene sheet shown in Fig. 7(b) because of high chemical potential of carbon on top of hump relative to a plain surface [20]. Due to the continuous deposition of hydrocarbon chemical radicals, the graphene sheets are continuously formed. In this case, the multi-layer graphene sheets are formed, which are depicted in Fig. 7(c). At the same time, the multi-layer graphene sheets and silicon substrate expand under the growth temperature. The difference in the expansion coefficients of graphene and Si results in the separation of one end of the multi-layer graphene sheets from the Si surface to form the CNWs shown in Fig. 7(d). A number of carbon nanodots mean that there are many nucleation positions of CNWs on the substrate surface; thus a number of CNWs are formed on the Si substrate deposited with the carbon nanodots. According to these analyses and the formation mechanism in Fig. 7, we can conclude that the CNWs become large if the density of carbon nanodots is low and they become small if the density of carbon nanodots is large, i.e., the growth of CNWs can be controlled through the controlling formation of carbon nanodots.

For the mirror-polished Si substrate, its surface is not automatically smooth and there are some micro-valleys [21]. Carbon first nucleates in these micro-valleys due to a low energy for nucleation [19], and then graphene sheets form depending on the carbon nuclei as shown in Fig. 7. However, some micro-valleys mean that there are a few nucleation positions for CNWs on the mirror-polished Si surface. As a result, only some CNWs are formed on the mirror-polished Si substrate.

Because carbon black can promote the decomposition of methane [22], the carbon nanodots can accelerate the decomposition of hydrocarbon radicals. According to the formation mechanism of CNWs shown in Fig. 7, there are carbon atoms to be formed on the surfaces of carbon nanodots so that the thickness of CNWs is increased. Thus, Fig. 3 shows that the CNWs grown on the Si substrate deposited with the carbon nanodots are thicker than that on the mirror-polished Si substrate.

Fig. 8 shows the PL spectra of CNWs formed on the Si substrate deposited with the carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate, in which every PL spectrum exhibits the strong blue and green PL peaks centered at about 442 and 549nm and a weak red PL peak centered at about 657nm. Simultaneously, Fig. 8 also shows that the intensity of every PL peak in spectrum (2) is stronger than that in spectrum (1). For the two sharp peaks at about 374 and 616nm, they do not belong to the PL peaks and may result from the instrument itself or the interference. The two PL peaks are observed for graphene oxide, but they are broad peaks and their center positions change with the structure of graphene oxide [9,10]. For our samples, the two sharp peaks have the same peak positions for spectra (1) and (2); thus they are not PL peaks.

The generation of PL peaks of CNWs and the difference in the intensity of PL peaks are related to the difference in the structures of thin and thick CNWs. In Refs. [9,10], the single PL peak of graphene oxide was observed; thus Eda et al. believed that the blue PL peak originates from the radiative recombination of electron–hole pairs generated in the localized states and Luo et al. suggested that the red PL peak results from the bandgap emission. However, our CNW samples generate multi-PL peaks, which indicate that the PL mechanism of CNSs is more complicated than that of graphene oxide. Fig. 6 indicates that the CNWs have sp3 carbon. Simultaneously, the strong D peaks in Fig. 3 indicate that the CNWs contain the definite sp3 carbon and defects [23]. Thus, a bandgap is formed in the CNW samples [10,24]. Due to the sp3 carbon, the π and π⁎ bands of the sp2 carbon lie within the bandgap of σ and σ⁎ bands of the sp3 carbon according to the state density model of carbon materials in Ref. [25]. The results in Ref. [9] indicate that the bandgap of sp2 carbon clusters is reduced with increase in size of sp2 carbon clusters and is about 0.5eV for the cluster with a diameter of ~3nm. Fig. 2 indicates that the thickness of CNWs is about 8nm and 5nm for the CNWs grown on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots and the mirror-polished Si substrate, which means that the bandgap is lower than 0.5eV. Thus, it is impossible that the PL peaks result from bandgap emission because the corresponding wavelength for a light with energy of 0.5eV is about 2486nm. For the radiative recombination of electron–hole pairs generated in the localized states, it occurs in the sp2 carbon cluster with a size of ~1nm [26]; thus the PL of CNWs cannot generate from the radiative recombination. The results of Fanchini et al. indicate that the energy difference of π⁎ and π states is ~2.5–3.5eV although the optical gap is lower than 1eV for carbon materials [27]. In Ref. [28] Iwano et al. used the state density model of carbon materials to suggest a PL mechanism, i.e., the PL bands in which the energy is more than 2eV can generate the transition between the π⁎ and π bands, or the transition between the σ⁎ band and the valence band formed by defects, while the transition between the π⁎ band and the valence band formed by defects occurs in the energy of lower than 2eV. For the three PL peaks, the corresponding energy is about 2.81, 2.26 and 1.89eV for 442, 549 and 657nm, respectively. Thus, the PL peaks centered at about 442 and 549nm can be attributed to the transition between the π⁎ and π bands. From Fig. 1, the size of carbon nanodots is diversified so that the thickness of CNWs is non-uniform according to the CNW formation mechanism shown in Fig. 7. As a result, the thin CNWs have a larger bandgap than the thick CNWs. Thus, the PL peaks centered at about 442 and 549nm are generated from the thin and thick CNWs, respectively. Fig. 6 indicates that the CNWs contain oxygen, which is related to the preparation process of CNWs. During the preparation of CNWs, it is inevitable to have some residual oxygen in the CVD reactor. In addition, the samples are not reserved in vacuum so that oxygen is absorbed on the surfaces of CNWs. Thus, a defect energy band related to oxygen atoms can form in the bandgap of π and π⁎ bands. In this case, the transition between the π⁎ band and the defect energy band related to oxygen atoms generates the PL peak centered about 657nm.

For the difference in the intensity of PL peaks, it is related to the defects and bandgap of CNWs. Because the CNWs grown on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots are thicker than that on the mirror-polished Si substrate, the former has more defects than the later. The defects such as dangling bonds play a role of quenching PL [29,30]. It is possible that the thick CNWs have many dangling bonds to lower the intensity of PL bands. In addition, the thick CNWs have a narrow bandgap relative to the thin CNWs, which makes the PL efficiency of thick CNWs low [29]. Thus, Fig. 8 shows that the intensity of every PL peak in spectrum (2) is stronger than that in spectrum (1).

The CNWs with different structures were grown on the mirror-polished Si substrate and the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots in the HFCVD system, where CH4 was used as the reactive gas. The structure and composition of synthesized CNWs were studied using FESEM, TEM, XPS and micro-Raman spectroscopy. The results indicate that the thick CNWs can be well grown on the Si substrate deposited with carbon nanodots while the thin CNWs are formed on the mirror-polished Si substrate. The formation of differently thick CNWs results from the different formation speeds of carbon atoms with and without carbon nanodots. The PL properties of CNWs were studied using photoluminescence spectroscopy. The two strong blue and green PL peaks centered at about 442 and 549nm originate from the transition between the π⁎ and π bands and the red PL peak centered at about 657nm is generated from the transition between the π⁎ band and the defect energy band related to oxygen atoms. The weak intensity of PL peaks for the thick CNWs compared with the thin CNWs is due to the low PL efficiency caused by the defects and narrow bandgap. The results are significant to control the structure of CNSs and contribute to the development of next optoelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China (cstc2012jjA40031)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure and Properties of Solid Surface
